---
title: "Chapter 10 — 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 09 · 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning](ch09.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [11 · 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection →](ch11.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

High-dimensional data is hard to look at, slow to model, and surprisingly empty — most of the space contains no data at all. Dimensionality reduction is the art of squeezing many features down to a few that still carry the signal, either to visualize data, denoise it, speed up downstream models, or expose the hidden structure (the *manifold*) that the raw coordinates obscure. It sits at the tail end of classical unsupervised learning and feeds almost everything else in the pipeline.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical unsupervised ML · used for visualization, compression, denoising, and faster modeling · the one key idea — real data of high nominal dimension usually lives near a low-dimensional surface, and we can find it.
:::

## 10.1 — Why reduce dimensions: the curse of dimensionality

Start with the intuition. In one dimension, 10 evenly spaced points cover the unit interval nicely. To cover a unit square with the same density you need 100 points; a unit cube needs 1000. In $d$ dimensions you need $10^d$. This explosion is the **curse of dimensionality**: as the number of features grows, the volume of the space grows exponentially, so any fixed dataset becomes hopelessly sparse.

Sparsity breaks the assumptions that many algorithms quietly rely on. Nearest-neighbor methods (Chapter 6) assume "close points are similar," but in high dimensions distances *concentrate* — the ratio between the nearest and farthest neighbor approaches 1, so "nearest" stops meaning much. Concretely, for points drawn uniformly in a $d$-cube, the gap between the minimum and maximum pairwise distance shrinks relative to the mean as $d$ grows:

$$ \lim_{d \to \infty} \frac{\text{dist}_{\max} - \text{dist}_{\min}}{\text{dist}_{\min}} \to 0. $$

When that ratio collapses, the very notion of a "closest" point dissolves, and any method that ranks neighbors by distance is ranking noise.

There are four everyday reasons to reduce:

- **Visualization** — humans see 2D and 3D; projecting to two dimensions lets you eyeball clusters and outliers.
- **Denoising** — keeping only the strongest directions discards small-variance noise.
- **Speed and memory** — fewer features mean faster training and smaller models downstream.
- **Beating the curse** — compact representations make distance- and density-based methods work again.

The saving grace is the **manifold hypothesis**: real data rarely fills its ambient space. A 100×100 grayscale image lives in a 10,000-dimensional space in principle, but the set of *natural face images* forms a thin, curved sheet — a low-dimensional **manifold** — inside it. As you slide along that sheet, the face rotates or smiles smoothly; the handful of knobs that drive those smooth changes are the manifold's true **intrinsic dimensionality**, far smaller than the 10,000 raw pixels. Dimensionality reduction tries to find coordinates *on* that sheet.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: if your features are correlated (and real-world features almost always are), the intrinsic dimensionality is far lower than the column count. That gap is exactly what these methods harvest.
:::

## 10.2 — Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

PCA is the workhorse. The intuition: find the direction in which the data varies the most, call it the first axis; then the direction of most remaining variation that is perpendicular to it, call it the second; and so on. Project onto the first few of these axes and you keep most of the spread while throwing away the rest.

Make it precise. Center the data so each feature has mean zero, giving matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$. The **covariance matrix** is

$$ C = \frac{1}{n-1} X^\top X. $$

A **principal component** is a unit vector $w$; the variance of the data projected onto it is $w^\top C\, w$. Maximizing that variance subject to $\|w\|=1$ is solved by the **eigenvectors** of $C$: the eigenvector with the largest **eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ is the first principal component, and $\lambda_i$ equals the variance captured along component $i$.

In practice nobody forms $C$ and eigendecomposes it — that squares the condition number and wastes memory. Instead use the **Singular Value Decomposition** of the centered data, $X = U\Sigma V^\top$. The columns of $V$ are exactly the principal components, and the singular values relate to eigenvalues by $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^2/(n-1)$. SVD is numerically stable and is what every library actually runs.

```python
import numpy as np
# X: (n, d) data matrix
Xc = X - X.mean(axis=0)              # center: PCA needs zero-mean columns
U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(Xc, full_matrices=False)
components = Vt[:k]                   # (k, d): top-k principal axes
Z = Xc @ components.T                # (n, k): data in reduced coords
explained = (S**2) / (S**2).sum()    # fraction of variance per component
```

**Choosing the number of components $k$** is usually done from the **explained variance ratio** $\lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$. Plot the cumulative sum and keep enough components to reach, say, 95%; or look for the "elbow" in the **scree plot** where eigenvalues flatten out.

The one easy mistake is scale. PCA maximizes raw variance, so a feature measured in millimeters will dominate the same feature in meters purely because its numbers are bigger. **Standardize** (z-score each column) whenever features are in different units — equivalently, run PCA on the correlation matrix instead of the covariance matrix. Skip standardization only when all features share a meaningful common scale (e.g. pixel intensities).

::: {.callout-warning}
PCA is a *linear* method and assumes variance equals information. If your signal lives in a low-variance direction (a faint but discriminative feature), PCA will happily delete it. PCA is unsupervised — it knows nothing about your labels.
:::

## 10.3 — A geometric picture of the projection

PCA rotates the coordinate axes to line up with the data's own spread, then drops the short axis. Below, a tilted elliptical cloud: PC1 follows the long axis (most variance), PC2 the short one. Projecting every point onto PC1 alone collapses 2D to 1D while preserving most of the spread.

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <ellipse cx="160" cy="110" rx="95" ry="38" transform="rotate(-25 160 110)" fill="none" stroke="#888" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
  <g fill="#4f8cff">
    <circle cx="95" cy="150" r="3"/><circle cx="120" cy="135" r="3"/><circle cx="140" cy="128" r="3"/>
    <circle cx="160" cy="112" r="3"/><circle cx="185" cy="100" r="3"/><circle cx="205" cy="92" r="3"/>
    <circle cx="225" cy="78" r="3"/><circle cx="130" cy="120" r="3"/><circle cx="175" cy="118" r="3"/>
    <circle cx="150" cy="100" r="3"/>
  </g>
  <line x1="80" y1="160" x2="240" y2="85" stroke="#e0533d" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="244" y="84" font-size="11" fill="#e0533d">PC1</text>
  <line x1="143" y1="92" x2="177" y2="128" stroke="#2faf6a" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="180" y="138" font-size="11" fill="#2faf6a">PC2</text>
</svg>

The red line is the axis the data is most "spread along"; the green line, orthogonal to it, captures what little variance is left. Discarding PC2 is a tiny error precisely because the cloud is thin in that direction.

## 10.4 — Kernel PCA: bending the linear method

PCA can only find flat (linear) structure. If your data sits on a curve — points spiraling outward, or two concentric rings — no straight axis separates them, and PCA fails. **Kernel PCA** fixes this with the same trick as kernel SVMs (Chapter 7): imagine mapping the data through a nonlinear feature map $\phi(x)$ into a much higher-dimensional space where the structure *is* linear, run ordinary PCA there, and never actually compute $\phi$.

The reason you can skip $\phi$ is the **kernel trick**: PCA only needs inner products, and a **kernel function** $k(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^\top \phi(x_j)$ gives those directly. You build the $n \times n$ kernel matrix $K$ (commonly the RBF kernel $k(x_i,x_j) = \exp(-\gamma\|x_i - x_j\|^2)$), center it, and eigendecompose *it* instead of a covariance matrix. The top eigenvectors give nonlinear principal components.

The cost is that you now work with an $n \times n$ matrix rather than $d \times d$, so kernel PCA scales poorly with the number of samples, and the result depends heavily on the kernel and its hyperparameters (e.g. $\gamma$). Use it when you suspect smooth nonlinear structure and $n$ is modest; reach for PCA first when in doubt.

## 10.5 — Independent Component Analysis (ICA)

PCA finds directions that are *uncorrelated* and maximize variance. ICA asks a different question: find directions that are *statistically independent*. The classic motivation is the **cocktail-party problem** — several microphones each record a mixture of several people talking at once, and you want to recover the individual voices. PCA would give you decorrelated mixtures; ICA recovers the actual sources.

The model assumes the observations are a linear mix of independent sources, $x = As$, and seeks an **unmixing matrix** $W \approx A^{-1}$ so that $s = Wx$ has independent components. The mathematical lever is **non-Gaussianity**: by the central limit theorem, a sum of independent signals looks *more* Gaussian than the originals, so making the recovered components as non-Gaussian as possible (maximizing **kurtosis** or **negentropy**, as the FastICA algorithm does) pulls the mixtures apart.

Two consequences fall out of the model. ICA cannot recover the *scale* or *sign* of a source (you can absorb a factor into $A$), nor their *order*. And it fundamentally cannot separate genuinely Gaussian sources — if everything is Gaussian, decorrelation is all you can achieve, and that is just PCA. ICA shines in signal processing: EEG/MEG artifact removal, audio separation, and finding interpretable latent factors that PCA's variance-ranking blurs together.

::: {.callout-tip}
A clean way to remember the split: PCA decorrelates (second-order statistics, variance), ICA makes independent (higher-order statistics, non-Gaussianity). Independence is the stronger condition — it implies decorrelation, but not the other way around.
:::

## 10.6 — Manifold learning: t-SNE and UMAP

When the goal is a 2D *picture* of complex data, linear projection rarely does it justice — a curled-up manifold needs to be "unrolled." **Manifold learning** methods learn nonlinear embeddings that preserve *local* neighborhood structure, and two dominate practice.

**t-SNE** (t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) turns pairwise distances into probabilities: for each point it asks "how likely would I pick you as a neighbor?" using a Gaussian in the original space, then arranges points in 2D so a heavy-tailed (Student-t) version of those probabilities matches as closely as possible, minimizing the KL divergence between the two. The heavy tail is the clever bit — it gives crowded points room in the low-dimensional map, separating clusters cleanly. Its main knob, **perplexity**, sets roughly how many neighbors each point tries to keep close (typical values 5–50); change it and the picture can change a lot.

**UMAP** (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) builds a fuzzy nearest-neighbor graph and optimizes a low-dimensional layout to preserve it. In practice it runs much faster than t-SNE, scales to millions of points, preserves more of the *global* structure, and can transform new unseen points (t-SNE generally cannot).

| Aspect | t-SNE | UMAP |
|---|---|---|
| Primary use | visualization | visualization + general reduction |
| Speed / scale | slow, ~10k–100k points | fast, millions of points |
| Global structure | mostly local | better preserved |
| Embeds new points | no (re-run) | yes (`transform`) |
| Key knob | perplexity | n_neighbors, min_dist |

Both share a dangerous property: **the geometry of the output is not literally meaningful.** Cluster *sizes* in a t-SNE/UMAP plot do not reflect real spread, the *distances between* clusters are largely arbitrary, and tweaking perplexity or `n_neighbors` can conjure or dissolve clusters. They are visualization aids, not metric spaces.

::: {.callout-warning}
Never feed raw t-SNE/UMAP coordinates into a downstream model as if they were real features, and never read "these two clusters are far apart, so they're very different" off the plot. Use these methods to *see*, then verify any structure with the original data. For modeling, prefer PCA or a learned encoder.
:::

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A[High-dimensional data] --> B{Goal?}
  B -->|Compress / denoise / speed| C[PCA or Kernel PCA]
  B -->|Separate mixed sources| D[ICA]
  B -->|2D picture for the eye| E[t-SNE / UMAP]
  C --> F[Reduced features for modeling]
  D --> G[Recovered independent signals]
  E --> H[Plot — inspect only, do not model on coords]
```

## 10.7 — Isomap, LLE, and autoencoders

Two earlier manifold methods are worth knowing for intuition. **Isomap** preserves *geodesic* distance — distance measured *along* the manifold rather than straight through space. Picture a rolled-up "Swiss roll": two points on opposite ends of the roll are close in 3D but far apart along the sheet. Isomap builds a neighbor graph, measures shortest paths through it (approximating geodesics), then finds a low-dimensional embedding matching those path distances. **Locally Linear Embedding (LLE)** takes a complementary view: each point is reconstructed as a weighted combination of its neighbors, and LLE finds low-dimensional coordinates that keep those same local reconstruction weights. Both capture nonlinear structure but are sensitive to neighborhood size and noise, and have largely been superseded by UMAP for visualization.

The modern generalization is the **autoencoder**: a neural network that squeezes input through a narrow **bottleneck** layer and reconstructs it, forcing the bottleneck to learn a compact code. This is dimensionality reduction *learned by gradient descent*, and a linear autoencoder with squared-error loss recovers the PCA subspace exactly — autoencoders are the nonlinear, trainable heir to PCA. They get their own full treatment in Chapter 19.

## 10.x — Key takeaways

- The **curse of dimensionality** makes high-dimensional space sparse and distances meaningless; the **manifold hypothesis** says real data still lives on a low-dimensional surface we can find.
- **PCA** maximizes variance along orthogonal directions = eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, computed in practice via **SVD**. Center always; **standardize** when features have different units.
- Choose components from the **explained-variance / scree** curve. PCA is linear, unsupervised, and can delete low-variance-but-informative signal.
- **Kernel PCA** adds nonlinearity via the kernel trick (costly at large $n$); **ICA** finds statistically independent sources via non-Gaussianity (cannot separate Gaussian sources, loses scale/order).
- **t-SNE** and **UMAP** make excellent 2D visualizations but their cluster sizes and inter-cluster distances are *not* meaningful — inspect, don't model on them. UMAP is faster, scales better, and embeds new points.
- **Isomap/LLE** preserve geodesic/local structure; **autoencoders** are the learned, nonlinear generalization of PCA.

## 10.y — See also

- **Chapter 02 — Mathematics for Machine Learning** (eigenvectors, SVD, covariance — the linear algebra PCA runs on)
- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering** (standardization and where reduction fits the pipeline)
- **Chapter 06 — Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers** (why the curse of dimensionality breaks nearest neighbors)
- **Chapter 07 — Support Vector Machines & Kernels** (the kernel trick reused by Kernel PCA)
- **Chapter 09 — Clustering & Unsupervised Learning** (the sibling task; reduction often precedes clustering)
- **Chapter 19 — Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning** (learned, nonlinear dimensionality reduction)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 09 · 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning](ch09.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [11 · 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection →](ch11.html)
